vocabulary.internet_text: The Internet is a global network that connects billions of computers, smartphones and devices all over the world. There are two main parts: 1) The Infrastructure: This is the hardware - the cables, satellites, routers and data centers that make the physical connections. 2) The Services: This is what we use every day – websites, apps, email, video calls and social media. To visit a website you need its address, called a URL (ex: www.example.com). You type this address into a browser (a program like Chrome or Safari) and it takes you there. How do you move online? You click on links to jump from one page to another. You use a search engine (like Google) to find information by typing keywords. To access private areas, you often need to log in with a username and password. The Internet is a powerful tool for learning, communication and work. It gives us access to endless information (data), allows us to download files to our devices and upload our own files to the cloud.
vocabulary.os_text: What happens when you turn on your computer or phone? How does it know what to do? How do you connect to a website that is stored on a computer far away? The Operating System (OS): The Main Manager. The Operating System (OS) is the most important software on your device. It's like a manager or a translator between you, your applications and the hardware. What does an OS do? 1. Manages Hardware: It controls the CPU, RAM, storage drives and other components. 2. Runs Applications: It allows you to open and use programs like your browser or word processor. 3. Manages Files and Folders: It creates a system of files and folders on your storage. Provides User Interface (UI): It gives you a way to interact with the device: a graphical interface (GUI) or a command line (CLI). Common Operating Systems: For PCs: Windows, macOS, Linux. For Smartphones: Android, iOS. For Servers: Often Linux distributions. Key Vocabulary: To boot (up) - to start up the computer and load the OS. Desktop/Laptop - personal computers. Server - a powerful computer that provides data or services. Task Manager/Activity Monitor - an OS tool to see running applications. Computer Networks: Connecting Devices. A computer network is a group of devices connected together to share resources. Why do we need networks? Share Internet connection, files, printers, access centralized data, communicate. Basic Network Types: 1. LAN (Local Area Network): small network in one location. 2. WAN (Wide Area Network): large network over big area. The Internet is the biggest WAN. IP Address: unique number for each device (ex: 192.168.1.5). Router: connects LAN to Internet, routes data. Bandwidth: maximum rate of data transfer. Key Vocabulary: To connect to network/Wi-Fi, stable/unstable connection, password-protected network, reset router.

true_false.statement: 1. Linux is only used for servers.
true_false.explanation: it's also used on PCs and devices

true_false.statement: 2. A LAN covers a larger area than a WAN.
3. Bandwidth refers to the physical width of a network cable.
